---
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# (PART\*) Spatial Modelling {.unnumbered}

# Interpolation {#interpolation}

## Introduction

![](images/banner-interpolation.png)

### Aims {.unnumbered}

The aims of this practical are to:

1.  Introduce geostatistical approaches for analysing spatial data.
2.  Understand how common spatial interpolation techniques work.
3.  Perform interpolation in ArcGIS and become familiar with common
    applications e.g., for prediction, as an input for spatial
    regression.

### Application {.unnumbered}

To achieve this, we'll apply interpolation techniques to Point samples
of global plant trait data (leaf area, plant height), modelled by
@lusk2026. Our study area is Peru, which features extreme gradients in climate and topography, which are likely to be reflected in spatial gradients in plant traits. 

### Data {.unnumbered}

This practical uses global plant trait data derived from crowdsourced
biodiversity monitoring and Earth observation, available in
[`data/global-plant-traits`]
[[Paper](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-68996-y)]
[[Source](https://global-traits.projects.earthengine.app/view/global-traits)]

### Tools {.unnumbered}

[Extract Values to
Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/extract-values-to-points.html?tabs=dialog)
(Spatial Analyst Tools),
[IDW](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geostatistical-analyst/idw.html?tabs=dialog)
(Geostatistical Analyst Tools), [Empirical Bayesian
Kriging](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geostatistical-analyst/empirical-bayesian-kriging.html?tabs=dialog)
(Geostatistical Analyst Tools), [Geostatistical
Wizard](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/help/analysis/geostatistical-analyst/get-started-with-geostatistical-analyst-in-arcgis-pro.html#9E3),
[Exploratory
Interpolation](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geostatistical-analyst/exploratory-interpolation.html?tabs=dialog)
(Geostatistical Analyst Tools)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Practical

Interpolation techniques are used to estimate unknown values at new
locations based on known measurements from surrounding sample points.

We could use our ecological dataset from Practical 5 here, but it's not
an ideal example, for a few reasons:

[1] Our Points don't have corresponding values that we would want to
predict i.e., characteristics of each observation [2] Our Points
represent individual observations, so to interpolate, we would have to
aggregate these in some way i.e., \# observations with a certain
distance of some representative locations (schematic?) [3]
Fundamentally, the distribution of Tiger observations is clearly
non-continuous, found mostly in designated reserves. Interpolation will
probably do a poor job at capturing this spatial pattern, and instead,
we'd probably want to use a species distribution model (Link to Spatial
Ecology) [4] As a final reason, our data are Presence-Only data (i.e.,
observations of Tigers), and we have no Absence-Data, see: -
<https://doi.org/10.1111/jbi.14622> -
<https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1472-4642.2011.00755.x>

**Instead**, we are going to apply interpolation to a dataset where we
assume the spatial pattern is continuous, and where we have a variable
we want to predict.

[1] Load leaf area dataset into ArcGIS, and Natural-Earth dataset

[2] Generate random points, within bounding geometry of Peru, excluding
marine-areas (100?)

-\> Minimum Bounding Geometry (envelop) -\> Clip to NE data -\> Create
Random Points

[3] Extract raster value [Extract Values to Points]

[4] Remove any rows where values are invalid (-9999) [A] Manually via
the Attribute Table [B] Select by Attribute (RASTERVALU \<\> -9999,
Export selection)

[5] We now have a valid dataset (all points within bounding geometry, on
land, with a SLA value). However, we are again doing geometry
calculations today (i.e., predicting values at locations), so we need to
working with data that is suitably projected, rather than using
geographic data.

-\> UTM 18S is probably an appropriate selection here -\> Use Project
tool

[6] First, IDW:

[Options] Effects of Power + search radius

----------------- General approach: Resources:
<https://doi.org/10.1111/jen.13455>,
<https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2021.109501>

----------------- Thoughts:

-\> Geostatistical Wizard! Talk through the separate tools, but do all
the calculations in the GW?

-\> Semi-variogram construction (and models)

-\> Focus on a subset of interpolation methods, but do them really well?
i.e., IDW + Kriging (non- and geostatistical) + Natural Neighbour

-\> Global vs. local, exact vs. approximate...

----------------- Tools:

[A] Inverse distance weighting

[B] Various forms of Kriging [Empirical Bayesian Kriging] -\> Universal?
[incorporating a trend function] -\> Ordinary? [assumption of
stationarity i.e., the statistical properties of the data do not change
across space. It assumes the underlying mean is constant but unknown]

```         
-> We should use universal, because we *would* expect non-stationarity i.e., trends in climate, elevation...
-> but we should run both
```

[C] Trend -\> Even weirder than Spline!

[D] Spline -\> Funky output!

[E] Natural Neighbour -\> Output constrained to Points (convex hull)

[NOT] co-kriging, uses one or more correlated secondary (auxiliary)
variables [NOT] any regression, RF, entropy methods

----------------- Finish

By comparing the interpolation output (e.g., Universal Kriging) with the
"true" values from the input data i.e., residual.

Need to do so cautiously, acknowledging that the input is also a model.

However, this is important for validation of interpolation outputs - how
well does our prediction match real empirical measurements?

Fundamentally, interpolation is important when we are studying something
that will expected to vary continuously through space (even if it goes
to 0) e.g., soil depth, elevation, species richness, but only have
measurements of that variable at discrete locations.

----------------- Alternative dataset ideas

Something like this (but not this):
<https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-024-03098-2>

AVONET - bird traits? <https://doi.org/10.1111/ele.13898>

TRY plant trait database <https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14904>

How about <https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-68996-y>, random
sampling..., pick a location with an interesting ecological gradient
(Peru, Iberia?)

ArcGIS raster issues [+32768]

----------------- Original approach

Need to go from Points to a variable at each location...

[1] Collect Events tool... dataset uncertainty / generalisation...

[1] Generate Near Table -\> Summarize -\> Join by ID

[1] Find Point Clusters - DBSCAN (50 km, minimum 10 observations)

[2] Case Field not working, because 'CLUSTER_ID' is double, not
integer...

[!!!] I don't think the tiger dataset is a great example because: [a] At
this scale, we really need a species distribution model [b] Tigers seem
to be mostly in the nature reserves, and any interpolation method will
struggle with that...

## Extra

## Resources

-   Oliver, M.A. and Webster, R.
    ([1990](https://doi.org/10.1080/02693799008941549)). Kriging: a
    method of interpolation for geographical information systems.
    International Journal of Geographical Information System, 4(3),
    pp.313-332.
-   Diggle, P.J., Tawn, J.A. and Moyeed, R.A.
    ([1998](https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9876.00113)). Model-based
    geostatistics. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series C:
    Applied Statistics, 47(3), pp.299-350.
-   Rufino, M.M., Albouy, C. and Brind'Amour, A.
    ([2021](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2021.109501)). Which
    spatial interpolators I should use? A case study applying to marine
    species. Ecological Modelling[MT3.1], 449, p.109501.
